Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I'd just drift through the week without actually sitting down to do it. When I did study, it was usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because my mind was racing with anxiety about everything I wasn't doing. I'd maybe get in a few hours here and there, but nothing consistent or planned out.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then when a deadline was breathing down my neck, I'd cram everything into a couple of days. It wasn't healthy, and I knew it wasn't working, but I just couldn't seem to get myself to do things differently. The anxiety made it hard to start, and then not starting made the anxiety worse.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in. I'd suddenly remember something was due or I'd get a notification, and that jolt of fear would push me to check the LMS. Sometimes I logged in just to see what I was behind on, which honestly just made me feel worse. Occasionally my peer support group would remind me about something, and that helped get me there too.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to that point where it felt automatic or normal. Every time I logged in, it felt like I was forcing myself to confront all the things I'd been avoiding. My peers tried to help me build a habit, and for maybe a week or two it worked, but then I'd slip back into my old patterns of avoidance.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually okay because I could see what other students were thinking and it made me feel less alone in being confused. The reading materials were useful when I actually engaged with them properly. I learn best through reading and writing things out, so when I took the time to actually process the text-based content, it stuck with me.
Q6: I basically ignored the videos most of the time. I know they were there and probably helpful, but sitting through them felt like such a commitment. I'd start watching and then my mind would wander to all the other things I should be doing, and I'd just close out. The optional practice activities also got skipped because I was always trying to catch up on required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to an assignment or exam. If it felt abstract or like busy work, I just couldn't motivate myself to care. When something clicked and I actually understood it, that felt worthwhile, but those moments were rare because I was usually rushing through everything.
Q8: Optional stuff might as well have not existed for me. I was barely keeping up with required activities, so anything labeled optional immediately got filed away as 'things I'll never get to.' Required activities at least had the threat of consequences, which was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my avoidance.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download readings and PDFs and then study from those on my tablet without being connected. I also have a lot of resources at home – good internet, a decent study space, books – so sometimes I'd use external materials to try to understand concepts I was struggling with. My notes were all handwritten because typing them didn't help me remember anything.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk things through with friends from my peer support group in person or over the phone, working through problems together. That kind of studying was actually more helpful than anything I did online, but it's invisible.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a lecture slide, look at it once in the LMS, and then spend days working through it offline. My LMS activity would show one quick access, but I might have read that document five or six times trying to understand it. The system can't see any of that continued effort.
Q12: There was a stretch in the middle of the semester where everything just felt impossible. My anxiety was through the roof, and I couldn't make myself do anything. I'd open my laptop intending to work and then just sit there feeling paralyzed. The peer support helped a bit, but I still missed a lot during that time and never fully caught up.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other life stuff, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Confusion was a big one too – if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help. I had other responsibilities pulling at me, and the course often lost that competition for my attention.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. It looked like engagement, but I was just going through the motions while my mind spiraled with worry.
Q15: Constantly. The bigger or more important something seemed, the more I avoided it. It's like my brain couldn't handle the weight of it, so it just shut down. I knew I was making things worse by waiting, but knowing that didn't help me actually do anything. The anxiety around starting was worse than the work itself usually turned out to be.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore everything until a deadline was imminent, then scramble to get something done. It wasn't good work usually, just whatever I could throw together in a panic. The LMS would show all my activity clustered right before due dates, which is pretty embarrassing to think about.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me for a day or two. If it was positive, I'd feel a little hope. If it was critical, I'd spiral and avoid the course for a while. The peer support people encouraged me to use feedback constructively, and that helped me not completely shut down when I got comments on my work.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming to me. There was so much content across all these courses, and I could never figure out what to prioritize. When the course had clear weekly expectations, that helped a little, but I still fell behind. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually made it easier for me to procrastinate.
Q19: Having everything online made it too easy to avoid. If I had to show up somewhere physically, I probably would have engaged more. The discussion forums were good because they felt lower stakes than assignments. But the sheer volume of materials and the way everything was spread across different sections made it hard to know where to focus.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. I'd log in, click around, maybe watch part of a video or read some discussion posts, but then I wouldn't actually complete the assignments. It's like I was trying to engage but couldn't follow through. The activity is there, but the output isn't, because something breaks down between intention and action.
Q21: I can see how that would happen if someone studies effectively offline. If you're good at independent learning and don't need the LMS except to submit work, your activity would be low but your performance could be fine. That wasn't me though – I needed more structure than I gave myself, and my low activity matched my low performance.
Q22: Logs can show when you were present and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually thinking or learning. They miss all the offline work, the conversations with peers, the mental struggles that prevent engagement. They see the surface but not what's underneath. My logs probably look like someone who didn't care, but I cared – I just couldn't act on it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually being mentally present and invested in what you're learning. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials – it's thinking about the content, wrestling with ideas, wanting to understand. For me, engagement was rare because my anxiety kept pulling me away even when I wanted to be present. True engagement felt like those brief moments when I actually forgot to be anxious.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I wanted to do better, or how hard the anxiety made everything. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that kept me from giving up entirely, or the offline studying I did manage. It also wouldn't show that my mood actually improved after getting support, even though my grades didn't reflect that. There's a whole emotional story behind those numbers that just doesn't translate into clicks and timestamps.
